content.text: (Command-Line Interface tools) are Python applications that expose functionality as
users run from the terminal. In Python packaging, CLI tools are created by defining
that map command names to Python functions.
What it is — Clear definition and context
A CLI tool is a Python package that, when installed, creates a

# Before installation (can't run)
my-cli --help  
# Error: command not found

# After: pip install my-cli-package
my-cli --help
# Works! Shows help text
Why it matters — Purpose and importance
CLI tools solve critical problems for software distribution:
: Integrate into shell scripts and automation
: Harder to debug installed CLI vs source

my_cli_project/
├── src/
│   └── my_package/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── cli.py           # CLI entry point
│       ├── core.py          # Business logic
│       └── utils.py         # Utilities
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
└── tests/
    └── test_cli.py
When to use it — Scenarios and decision criteria

# Initialize with uv (recommended 2026)
uv init --package my-tool
cd my-tool

my-tool/
├── src/
│   └── my_tool/
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── main.py
├── pyproject.toml
└── README.md

# src/my_tool/main.py

import argparse

def main():
    """Main CLI entry point"""
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        description="My CLI tool for doing things"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "name",
        help="Name to greet"
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--verbose",
        "-v",
        action="store_true",
        help="Enable verbose output"
    )
    
    args = parser.parse_args()
    
    if args.verbose:
        print(f"[Verbose] Greeting for: {args.name}")
    
    print(f"Hello, {args.name}!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=61.0"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "my-tool"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A CLI tool for doing things"
requires-python = ">=3.9"
authors = [
    {name = "Your Name", email = "you@example.com"}
]

# CLI ENTRY POINTS - This makes it a CLI tool!
[project.scripts]
my-tool = "my_tool.main:main"

[tool.setuptools]
packages = ["my_tool"]
flag creates editable install—changes update instantly without reinstalling

# Test with arguments
my-tool John

# Test with flags
my-tool John --verbose

# Test help
my-tool --help

# Install build tools
pip install build twine

# Build package
python -m build

# Upload to PyPI
twine upload dist/*
After publishing, anyone can install with:

# src/my_cli/cli.py

import argparse

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Simple CLI")
    parser.add_argument("name", help="Your name")
    parser.add_argument("--age", type=int, help="Your age")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    
    print(f"Hello, {args.name}!")
    if args.age:
        print(f"You are {args.age} years old.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

$ my-cli Alice
Hello, Alice!

$ my-cli Bob --age 30
Hello, Bob!
You are 30 years old.

- Auto-generated help text
- Better error messages
- Subcommand support
- Color output
Real-world example — Production Generative AI CLI

# Scaffold new project
genai-scaffold my-llm-app --template llm

# Setup provider
genai-scaffold setup --provider anthropic

# Lair provides feature-rich chat interface from command line
lair chat
lair --mode prompt-engineering
lair comfy generate-image prompt="cat"
Always include guard for CLI entry point

- Type validation 
- Subcommand support 
- Color output
Comparison with alternatives — CLI vs Other Interfaces
: Use environment variables, not CLI args
CLI tools expose Python functions as terminal commands
: Better than argparse for complex tools
For Generative AI software engineering, CLI tools make your AI functionality accessible from the terminal, scriptable in automation workflows, and easy to distribute to other developers.
